entry.title: The user's s/4hana public cloud system display is inconsistent with the knowledge "3111463".
entry.content:
Hello expert,

3111463 - In Manage Product Master Data, search for a product which is Marked for Deletion and gives "Invalid entry" (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition all versions; SAP S/4HANA Cloud all versions)

A product marked for deletion should not be available for further usage outside the Manage Product Master Data application. Thus these products are unavailable for selection via the search help/value help.

But currently, it is not working as per design. In the user's s/4hana public cloud, the product data with deletion mark still was shown when using select material via the search help/value help. This is inconsistent with the knowledge “3111463". 

Do you think this is a bug in the user's system? How can I correct it? Thanks!

eg:1000012520 , has already was marked for deletion, but this product data still can be displayed via search help/value help.

Best regards,

Wendy

entry.title: Re: Single WF in SAP Data Services
entry.content:
Try to run a very simple job. In trace, you will see similar info like

  • (14.3) 09-19-24 05:57:22 (6688:5708) JOB: Job <JB_974401> is started.
  • (14.3) 09-19-24 05:57:23 (8652:6172) DATAFLOW: Process to execute data flow <New_DataFlow> is started.

6688 and 8652 are PID of al_engine. When job is running, check your DS server machine -> Task manager, there are running process as al_engine.

entry.title: Gain smart insights from your business data by integrating Generative AI Hub with PandasAI
entry.content:
Introduction 

The implementation of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is becoming more popular as a technique to provide accurate and reliable responses from Large Language Models (LLMs). This time, we examine PandasAI as a method to enable LLMs to handle more advanced tasks such as data analysis. PandasAI allows data search and visualization by generating Python code for data analysis in response to natural language queries. In this article, we explore how PandasAI can be integrated with Generative AI Hub, enabling developers to quickly create a chat application to interact with their business data using only Python.

Prerequisites

When using Generative AI Hub for the first time to access Language Models (LLMs), refer to the following document for a quick start:

https://pypi.org/project/generative-ai-hub-sdk/

 

from dotenv import load_dotenv
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.core.proxy_clients import get_proxy_client

load_dotenv(override=True)
proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub')
model_name = 'gpt-4o'
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name=model_name, proxy_client=proxy_client)

 

In the following document, you can find the models available in Generative AI Hub. Please note that you must deploy the model you want to use, such as GPT-4o, as described in the document.

https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-ai-core/sap-ai-core-service-guide/create-deployment-for-generative-ai-model-in-sap-ai-core

More details on how to deploy them are covered in steps 2 to 4 of the following tutorial:

https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/ai-core-generative-ai.html

Smart Excel Chat

Preparation (Back-end)

PandasAI class manages Excel file uploads and facilitates chat interactions based on their contents. Upon initialization, it creates two empty lists: files for storing DataFrames and filenames for the corresponding file names. The upload_excel method takes a list of file paths, reads each Excel file into a DataFrame, and appends them to files while also storing the file names in filenames. It returns a string of the uploaded file names for display in the interface.

 

import os
import pandas as pd
from typing import List, Unio

class PandasAI:
    def __init__(self):
        self.files = []
        self.filenames = []

    def upload_excel(self, file: List[str]) -> str:
        try:
            for filepath in file:
                df = pd.read_excel(filepath)
                self.files.append(df)
                self.filenames.append(os.path.basename(filepath))
            return "\\n".join(self.filenames)
        
        except Exception as e:
            return f"An error occurred: {str(e)}"

 

The chat_with_excel method allows users to interact with uploaded data through messages while maintaining a chat history. The Agent class from PandasAI facilitates interaction with dataframes for multi-turn conversations. This method is designed to handle both image and text outputs. Since the Agent outputs dataframes and numbers (not just strings), they are cast to strings in lines 29 and 31.

 

from pandasai import Agent

class PandasAI:
    def __init__(self):
        self.files = []
        self.filenames = []

    def upload_excel(self, file: List[str]) -> str:
        # Code omitted for brevity

    def chat_with_excel(self, message: str, chat_history: List) -> List[Union[str, List]]:
        agent = Agent(self.files, config={"llm": llm})
        prompt = f'''            
            User Message:
            {message}
            
            Chat History:
            {str(chat_history)}
        '''
        bot_message = agent.chat(prompt)

        if isinstance(bot_message, str) and os.path.exists(bot_message):                    
            img = gr.Image(bot_message)
            chat_history.append((message, None))
            chat_history.append((None, img))
            return ["", chat_history]

        elif isinstance(bot_message, pd.DataFrame):
            bot_message = bot_message.to_string(index=False)
        else:
            bot_message = str(bot_message)

        chat_history.append((message, bot_message))
        return ["", chat_history]

 

Preparation (Front-end)

We prepared the UI using Gradio, a Python library that allows developers to quickly create ML-based applications. 

 

import gradio as gr

with gr.Blocks() as excel_chatbot:
    gr.Markdown("## Smart Excel Chat")
    
    with gr.Row():
        with gr.Column():
            excel_file = gr.File(label="Upload Excel Sheet", file_count="multiple", file_types=[".xlsx"], type="filepath")
            upload_button = gr.Button("Upload")
            upload_status = gr.Textbox(label="Uploaded Files", interactive=False)
        with gr.Column():
            chatbot = gr.Chatbot()
            msg = gr.Textbox()
            clear = gr.ClearButton([msg, chatbot])
    
    pandasai = PandasAI()
    
    def reset_file():
        return None

    upload_button.click(pandasai.upload_excel, inputs=excel_file, outputs=upload_status)
    upload_button.click(reset_file, outputs=excel_file)

    msg.submit(
        pandasai.chat_with_excel,
        inputs=[msg, chatbot],
        outputs=[msg, chatbot]
    )

excel_chatbot.launch()

 

It includes components for uploading Excel sheets, a chat view, and a text input box.

Performance Verification

I will demonstrate our prototype using the following sample data related to HR. We prepared two simple Excel sheets for the demonstration, even though it worked well with larger datasets as well.

The app can correctly answer user queries by considering the chat history.

It can plot bar chart,

and pie chart as well.

Limitations

When we ask a query about something the LLMs shouldn't know, they hallucinate.

However, it recognizes when it lacks the necessary information. By restructuring the prompt to ensure that the LLM has access to the relevant information and adjusting actions based on the output, we assume that this issue can be addressed.

Conclusion

Integrating PandasAI with Generative AI Hub enhances data analysis by enabling seamless interaction with business data through natural language queries. This solution simplifies tasks like data search and visualization, making them more accessible and intuitive. It demonstrates the powerful potential of RAG to improve productivity and streamline workflows in business applications.

entry.title: Upgrading SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - Why, How, and Best Practices guide - Version 4
entry.content:
Considering upgrading to SAP S/4HANA 2023 or higher?

The latest version of our well-known supplementary guide is now released. You can use this guide to:

  • Build your business case for upgrade
  • Plan your upgrade
  • Execute your upgrade - supplementing the official SAP S/4HANA upgrade guide for your release + solution suite
  • Get more out of your SAP S/4HANA solution post-upgrade

Now including even more lessons learned from real customers, latest tooling to make your upgrades more efficient, plus pragmatic advice on what to do with upgrade versus post-upgrade. 

Hint: Our most important recommendation is to do some pre-planning of post-upgrade adoption. This makes sure your technical upgrade has considered any critical architecture prerequisites to smooth your post-upgrade plans, so that you gain the promised business value of your new SAP S/4HANA release. 

You can download the guide here:

Upgrading SAP S/4HANA - Why, How, and Best Practices

What's new in Version 4

The guide is now focussed on:

  • Solution suite SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, with some additional comments for SAP S/4HANA on-premise customers.
  • Project approaches Technical Upgrade and Technical Upgrade Plus (i.e. with carefully chosen urgent new business value) 
  • Release SAP S/4HANA 2023 and higher 
  • Clean Core - what's mandatory for your upgrade 
  • Recommended tooling to make your upgrades more efficient - including SAP Signavio, SAP CALM, SAP Activate, and more
  • Advice on SAP Services that may help you manage your upgrade
  • Known issues and FAQs
  • Practical advice on what major shifts to include with your upgrade versus defer to post-upgrade

The guide has been restructured to clarify what activities to include in your Plan, Execute, and Post-Upgrade. 

In the Plan chapter:

For those considering using their upgrade as an opportunity to deal with major shifts - including compatibility packs expiring end of 2025, or end-of-maintenance SAP Business Suite solutions expiring end of 2027 - we have added a series of Special Considerations.  The aim is to give you the critical notes and references to start evaluating your options, assess business impact, and decide whether to make the shift with upgrade or post-upgrade. 

In the Execute chapter:

We have included mandatory activities and recommended activities for technical upgrades. Recommended activities are those that improve the efficiency of your upgrades and of your business-as-usual support. 

In the Post-Upgrade chapter: 

We have added some advice to get you started on introducing new business value whether you want to:

  • Move to Clean Core
  • Introduce new business processes
  • Introduce new processes powered by SAP Business AI
  • Introduce new user experiences - more apps, more intelligent experiences, central launchpad on SAP Build Zone, mobile devices and/or wearables, transition users from SAP GUI to SAP Fiori, and more
  • Make important shifts that help you transition your users to innovation over time, such as move from groups to spaces and pages

And finally there are updated learning recommendations for users, functional consultants, UX architects, developers, and administrators. 

We hope you find the guide useful for your upgrade planning... and we always welcome feedback!

Important: This is intended to be the final version of this guide (we may perhaps make some minor corrections).  Future advice is being collected into the SAP Activate roadmaps for upgrading SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, and upgrading SAP S/4HANA on-premise.

Looking for more ... ?

You’ll find much more on our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition topic page and  SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA topic page

We also recommend you follow tags SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA for more blogs and updates.

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG and Customer Care team.

entry.title: A Brief History of SAP: An Introduction for the Uninitiated
entry.content:
SAP, short for Systems, Applications, and Products in Data Processing, is a global leader in enterprise resource planning (ERP) software. Founded in 1972, SAP has transformed how businesses manage their operations, finances, and resources.

Note:  This article is for someone unfamiliar with SAP. It explores the high-level history of SAP, covering its evolution, major milestones, and its impact on the business world over the last five decades. It's ideal for anyone looking to learn basic history and understand key developments.

The Early Years: 1972-1980

SAP was founded in Germany by five former IBM employees. The initial goal was to create a standard software solution to manage business processes. In 1973, they released their first product, SAP R/1, an integrated financial accounting system. This software was revolutionary for its time, allowing companies to process data in real-time and share information across departments.

Growth and Expansion: 1980s

As businesses recognized the need for integrated systems, SAP began to grow rapidly. In 1981, SAP introduced SAP R/2, which expanded its capabilities to support manufacturing, sales, and distribution processes. The software gained popularity among large enterprises in Germany and beyond.

By the mid-1980s, SAP was well-established in the German market and started expanding internationally. The company opened its first office outside Germany in the United States in 1988, marking the beginning of its global footprint.

The Birth of R/3: 1992

In 1992, SAP launched SAP R/3, a landmark product that transitioned the company from mainframe-based systems to a client-server architecture. R/3 provided a more flexible and user-friendly interface, allowing organizations to access and manage data across various functions, including finance, HR, and logistics.

R/3's success marked a significant turning point for SAP, as it enabled the company to capture a substantial share of the growing ERP market. By the late 1990s, SAP R/3 became synonymous with ERP solutions, helping businesses streamline their operations and improve efficiency.

SAP ECC 5.0 and ECC 6.0: 2000s

In the early 2000s, SAP introduced SAP ECC 5.0 (Enterprise Central Component) in 2004, which provided enhanced functionalities and improved integration capabilities over the previous R/3 version. ECC 5.0 emphasized process integration and included features such as enhanced business analytics and better user interfaces.

In 2006, SAP released SAP ECC 6.0, which became the foundation for many organizations looking to upgrade their ERP systems. ECC 6.0 offered significant improvements in performance, scalability, and user experience, integrating various modules for finance, supply chain, human resources, and more. This version marked a critical period in SAP's evolution, as it provided companies with tools to manage their entire operations efficiently.

Expanding Portfolio: SAP Business One, SuccessFactors, Fieldglass and many more

To cater to a broader market, SAP introduced several new products in the 2000s and 2010s:

  • SAP Business One: Launched in 2002, SAP Business One is designed for small to medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). It offers an integrated solution that helps manage business operations, including finance, sales, and customer relationships, allowing smaller organizations to benefit from ERP functionalities.

  • SuccessFactors: Acquired by SAP in 2012, SuccessFactors is a cloud-based human capital management (HCM) solution. It helps organizations manage various HR functions, including recruitment, performance management, and employee development, aligning workforce management with business strategy.

  • SAP Fieldglass: Also acquired in 2014, SAP Fieldglass specializes in managing external workforce and services procurement. It provides organizations with tools to manage contingent labor and services procurement, enhancing visibility and control over external resources.

SAP Modules: A Brief Overview

SAP offers a wide range of modules (65) that cater to various business functions. Some of the key modules include:

  1. SAP Financial Accounting (FI): Manages financial transactions and reporting.
  2. SAP Controlling (CO): Supports internal cost reporting and analysis.
  3. SAP Sales and Distribution (SD): Handles order processing, sales, and distribution logistics.
  4. SAP Material Management (MM): Manages procurement and inventory processes.
  5. SAP Production Planning (PP): Supports manufacturing and production processes.
  6. SAP Human Capital Management (HCM): Manages employee data, payroll, and recruitment.
  7. SAP Customer Relationship Management (CRM): Manages interactions with customers and prospects.
  8. SAP Supply Chain Management (SCM): Optimizes supply chain processes, including planning and logistics.
  9. SAP Business Intelligence (BI): Provides reporting and analytics capabilities for better decision-making.
  10. SAP Project System (PS): Manages project planning, execution, and control.

These modules can be integrated to provide a comprehensive solution tailored to an organization's specific needs.

New Millennium: SAP’s Innovations

Continuing its innovative streak, SAP introduced SAP ERP in 2004, further expanding its suite of applications. In 2006, SAP launched SAP Business Suite, which included various applications tailored for different industries, solidifying its position as a leader in the ERP market. This period also marked the beginning of SAP's focus on integrating analytics, business intelligence, and mobile capabilities into its offerings.

The Shift to Cloud: 2010s

As the business world shifted toward cloud computing, SAP recognized the need to adapt. In 2012, SAP launched SAP HANA, an in-memory database and computing platform that allowed businesses to analyze data in real-time, enabling quicker decision-making and improved operational efficiency.

SAP also embraced the cloud model, launching SAP S/4HANA in 2015. This next-generation ERP suite combined the capabilities of SAP HANA with a user-friendly interface and flexible deployment options, including on-premise and cloud solutions. S/4HANA represented a significant evolution in SAP's offerings, catering to modern business needs.

Recent Developments: 2020s and Beyond

In recent years, SAP has continued to innovate, focusing on sustainability, machine learning, and artificial intelligence. The company has emphasized helping businesses transition to more sustainable practices and integrate advanced technologies into their operations.

SAP's acquisition of companies like Qualtrics in 2019, a leader in experience management, reflects its commitment to delivering comprehensive solutions that address customer needs across various dimensions—product, customer, employee, and brand.

SAP Today: A Publicly Traded Leader

SAP is a publicly traded company, listed on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) under the ticker symbol SAP. As one of the largest software companies in the world, SAP generates billions in annual revenue and serves thousands of clients across diverse industries. Its extensive portfolio includes not only ERP solutions but also products for customer experience, supply chain management, and human capital management.

SAP's solutions are utilized by many large organizations, including global giants like Coca-Cola, BMW, and Unilever, as well as privately held companies like Bosch, Cargill, and Koch Industries. The company also serves government clients, including the U.S. Department of Defense, NASA, and the United Nations. This diverse customer base demonstrates the reliability and scalability of SAP's offerings and underscores its reputation in various industries. The ability to serve both large enterprises and small-to-medium businesses (SMBs) through products like SAP Business One illustrates SAP's comprehensive market approach.

SAP has strategically expanded its operations worldwide, establishing key offices in regions such as North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. This global footprint allows SAP to cater to local markets while leveraging international resources and expertise. The company emphasizes partnerships with local firms to enhance its service delivery and customer engagement in these regions.

In terms of market position, SAP competes with other major players such as Oracle, Microsoft, and Workday. While Oracle is a strong competitor in the ERP space, particularly for large enterprises, SAP maintains a significant market share, especially in industries such as manufacturing and logistics. Microsoft, through its Dynamics 365 platform, offers integrated solutions that appeal to a wide range of businesses, while Workday focuses primarily on HCM and financial management solutions.

SAP's ongoing commitment to innovation and customer success positions it as a leader in the ERP and business software markets, making it a key player in shaping the future of enterprise technology.

Conclusion

Today, SAP is a dominant player in the ERP software market, serving thousands of organizations worldwide across various industries. With a rich history spanning over five decades, SAP has continually evolved to meet the changing demands of businesses. Its journey from a small German startup to a global leader exemplifies the importance of innovation, adaptability, and a commitment to customer success.

For those unfamiliar with SAP, understanding its history provides valuable context for its role in shaping the modern business landscape. As organizations continue to seek integrated solutions for managing their operations, SAP's legacy and future innovations will undoubtedly play a significant role in their success.

I wrote this article because family and friends often ask me what SAP is when I mention I've worked with this ERP throughout my career. I aimed to keep it simple and easy to understand, even for those unfamiliar with the topic. If any enhancements or corrections are needed, feel free to message me privately, and I'll be happy to review and update it as required.

entry.title: Migration:- SAP HANA Database copy/move of tenant database
entry.content:
Hi SAP, 

We are Migrating our on-Premise HANA database to Cloud Azure.  Below are more details. 
*********************************************************************************************************************>
On-premises setup: Multi node(3 hosts),Standalone HANA DB with XSA on separate tenant DB.

Nodes: 
node1, node2, node3

Tenant DB’s:
BHP - System DB
BHP – XSA installed
GHP - Client Data

Versions:
HANA:           :2.00.059.1713941539
XSA             :v1.1.3.230717145654
OS              :RHEL7.9


CLOUD Setup: Single Node, Standalone PERSON_NAME DB with XSA on separate tenant DB.

Tenant DB’s:
BHP - System DB
BHP – XSA installed
GHP - Client Data

Versions:
HANA:           :SAP HANA 2 SPS07 Revision 075.00
XSA             :v1.1.8.240122150851
OS              :RHEL9
*********************************************************************************************************************

And, was going through all possible Data migration approach & found below SAP document. On top of this have few questions. 

Copying and Moving Tenant Databases:-

 

Questions:- (If possible kindly do share SAP document/Note to justify answer)

1. Copy/Paste from Multi Node Source DB to Single Node target HANA, will it support ?

2. Copy/Paste for XSA tenant DB also support ? If yes is there any version dependency, like Target should be same as source or Target should be Higher ?

3. How much time will it take to copy/move 1 TB of DB ? (Approx)

4. After one time copy/move, will it act as real time replication (Apply all changes from source to Target) until we run below command ? ==> ALTER DATABASE <new_database_name> FINALIZE REPLICA

5. Is there any OS dependency or restriction for this Copying and Moving Tenant Databases. 
   Source RHEL 7.9 to Target RHEL 9

Thanks & Regards


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Migration:- SAP HANA Database copy/move of tenant dat...
entry.content:
Dear customer,

Thank you for contacting SAP Support.
Please refer to the answers below.

1. Copy/Paste from Multi Node Source DB to Single Node target HANA, will it support ?
A: Yes, it's supported.

2. Copy/Paste for XSA tenant DB also support ? If yes is there any version dependency, like Target should be same as source or Target should be Higher ?
A: Please refer to SAP Note 3445668 - Recommendations Regarding System Copy with SAP HANA XS Advanced. For further support, you may open a separate case with BC-XS-RT component.

3. How much time will it take to copy/move 1 TB of DB ? (Approx)
A: As you may have guessed, it's not possible to provide you the expected elapse time for this operation as it depends on the network bandwidth.

4. After one time copy/move, will it act as real time replication (Apply all changes from source to Target) until we run below command ? ==> ALTER DATABASE <new_database_name> FINALIZE REPLICA
A: Yes, right. 
So, you have to run that command 'ALTER DATABASE <new_database_name> FINALIZE REPLICA'.
Please be informed that the it is impossible to login before manually run the finalize sql.

5. Is there any OS dependency or restriction for this Copying and Moving Tenant Databases. 
   Source RHEL 7.9 to Target RHEL 9
A: With that environment, it's fine because these OS versions are all supported with the respective HANA versions. Please take a look at KBA 2235581 - SAP HANA: Supported Operating Systems

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: 初心者から始めるクラウドサービスのSAML認証
entry.content:
本ブログは、SAML認証の初心者の方を対象に技術的な解説をすることを目的としています。例えば、シングルサインオン(SSO)の基本をキャッチアップしたい方、会計コンサルタントやSAP インフラ/Basis担当の皆さんを主な対象として書いています。

 

はじめに

近年、従来のオンプレミスを中心としたアーキテクチャーからクラウドやWebアプリケーション中心のアーキテクチャーになってきています。従来のインフラ Basisだけではなく、SaaS製品の構築／運用する必要が出てくる中で、業務コンサルタントやインフラBasisエンジニアもクラウドサービスの技術をカバーする必要があるケースも多くなっています。

そこで問題になるのが認証です。

認証は従来から必要ですが、形が変わってきています。例えば、従来のSAP技術におけるSAP GUIやSAP Logon Ticket、Kerberos認証はイントラネットからのアクセスが前提にあったり、会社が管理している端末からのアクセスが前提にあったりしります。これは、クラウドサービスにおけるインターネットやモバイルからのアクセスには必ずしも適していません。そこで、クラウドサービスで業界標準となっておりますSAML認証を理解する必要があります。また、クラウドサービス導入はS4HANA Cloudをはじめ、IT部門ではなく、会計部門主導でシステムを入れることも多く、業務コンサルタントが認証技術の知識も必要になっているケースも見られています。

 

SAML認証のアーキテクチャー

SAML認証には、Identity Provider (IdP) とService Provider (SP)という２つのシステムが必要になります。

IdPは認証基盤と呼ばれるシステムであり、具体的にはSAP Identity Authentication (IAS) 、SAP SSOやMicrosoft Entra ID などの製品が該当します。IdPは認証を司っており、ID/Passwordやその他の方法で実際に認証を許可したり、多要素認証やIPアドレス制限といった追加の認証要件を実現したりする役割を持っています。

一方、SPは実際に使用する業務アプリケーションであり、具体的にはS/4HANA CloudやBTP、SuccessFactorsなどの製品が該当します。SPはアプリやその利用を認可するための権限管理/ロール管理を実現する役割を持っています。

 

 

一般的に、IdPとSPのそれぞれにユーザが存在し、それぞれ管理が必要となりますそれぞれユーザがいるというと二重管理に思えますが、、全く同じ情報を管理しているわけではありません。IdPはパスワードなど、認証に関する情報を管理し、SPはロールなど、認可や権限に関するが情報を管理します。両方のユーザを管理するうえで注意することは、IdPとSPでName ID（e.g. EmailやUser ID、従業員番号）を一致させる必要があることです。一致させないと両システム間でユーザを特定することができずログインすることができません。そのため、IdP側とSP側で何のName IDを一致させるか事前に設計/確認することが必要です。

※ユーザ情報の二重管理を避けるために、SPによっては、例えば、SACのDynamic User CreationやBTPのRole Collection Mappingなどの機能がある場合もあります。

 

SAML認証の認証フロー

SAML認証では、SPとIdPでアサーションと呼ばれる認証情報をやりとりして、ログインを許可するか却下するかといった判断をおこないます。ただし、ここでSPとIdPは直接やりとりする訳ではなく、アサーションをユーザ端末のブラウザを経由してやり取りすることでＳＳＯ（認証）を実現します。

※SAML認証にはBack ChannelというSPとIdPが直接やりとりする方法もありますが、メジャーな方法ではないため、ここでは割愛します。

認証フローをステップごとに説明していきます。

  1. ユーザは端末のブラウザからSPにアクセスします
  2. SPはアサーションでSAML認証要求を出し、ユーザをIdPにリダイレクトします。（多くのSPではユーザ操作なしに自動でリダイレクトします。）
  3. ユーザの端末のブラウザを経由してアサーションでSAML認証要求をIdPに転送します。ここでユーザはID/Passwordやその他の方法でIdPへログインします。
  4. ユーザがログインに成功すると、IdPはアサーションでSAML認証応答を出し、ユーザをSPへリダイレクトします。
  5. ユーザの端末のブラウザを経由してアサーションでSAML認証応答をSPに転送します。
  6. SPへのログイン(SSO)に成功します。

※SPのName ID とSAML認証応答の中で送られるIdPのName IDは一致していることが必要です。よくある設計/設定ミスとして、IdPの認証応答のName IDには従業員番号が入っているが、SPはEmailアドレスを受け取る設定になっているというような不整合が良くあります。

また、全ての要求/応答はSPとIdPで直接やり取りされず、必ずユーザの端末のブラウザ経由でやり取りされていることに注意して下さい。この特徴により、オンプレの認証基盤でクラウドサービスにログインする、あるいはその逆も実現することができます。

 

SAML認証の設定ステップ

SAML認証を設定/実装するためには、主に以下の３ステップが必要です。

１．IdP設定

IdPにSPの名前やURL、証明書を登録します。多くのSPではMetadataをダウンロードできるため、そのMetadataをIdPにアップロードすることで登録を自動化できます。また、アサーションの中で使うName IDに何を使用するかといった設定も実施します。
IPアドレス制限や多要素認証といった追加のセキュリティ設定がある場合もここで設定を行います。

２．SP設定

逆にSPにIdPの名前やURL、証明書を登録します。IdPでもMetadataをダウンロードできるため、そのMetadataをSPにアップロードすることで登録を自動化できます

３．ユーザ/権限作成

SPとIdPの両方でユーザ登録、権限登録を行います。ユーザ情報の中のNameIDに使う項目がきちんと一致するようなユーザを必ず登録することに注意してください。また、権限の作成/付与はSPで行ってください。

※これらのステップはあくまで参考であり、実際は製品固有の設定が必要になるケースもあります。詳細は各製品Helpを参照ください。

 

本ブログではここまでSAML認証の基本事項を説明してきました。
今回は一般的で基本的な事項を中心に解説しましたので、今後は特定の製品に特化した具体的で詳しい情報をBlog化していこうと考えています。

entry.title: Create infoset based on indirect join
entry.content:
Hi there,

I don't have much ABAP knowledge but I need to create a infoset which joins VIBDOBJASS-OBJNRTRG and IFLOS-TPLNR. 

The problem is, OBJNRTRG has this short 2 letter string at the front of the entry which indicates the type of object (its like 2 concatenated fields)

To join on IFLOS-TPLNR, I would need to remove this 2 letter string.

Here's an example

VIBDOBJASS-OBJNRTRG

IF?0000000000000xxxxx

IFLOS-TPLNR

?0000000000000xxxxx

 

How can I make such a query? Do I create an additional field with that in the record processing code? could you provide some guidance how this would actually be structured technically?

entry.title: Re: Full and partial Pallet Picking from same bin.
entry.content:
Hi Community,
Is following approach to solution is correct -

To achieve the desired picking strategy in SAP EWM, we can use quantity classifiers for different units of measure (UOM) and configure your Storage Type Search Sequence (STSS) accordingly. Here’s how we can do it:

  1. Assign Quantity Classification to UOM:

    • Go to SPRO path: Define Unit of Measure Attributes.
    • Assign the classification ‘P’ to the UOM PAL (Pallet).
    • Assign the classification ‘C’ to the UOM CS (Cases).

  2. Maintain UOM in Material Master:

    • Define UOM record for PAL and Cases in the material master.

  3. Create Records in STSS:

    • Create two records in the Storage Type Search Sequence (STSS):
      • One with classification ‘P’ for your storage type for full pallets.
      • One with classification ‘C’ for same storage type for full Cases.
      • One with a blank classification for the same storage type designated for partial pallets/cases.

  4. Define Access Sequences for STSS:

    • Set up access sequences for the STSS to first check with the classification and then without the classification.

By following these steps, we can ensure that the system prioritizes picking full pallets first and then partial pallets from the same bin, aligning with the requirement.
Please let me know if it's going to work. Thanks for reading this out.

entry.title: PL by Segment
entry.content:
Would any one help me to understand what's the problem with the below query are:

/* SELECT FROM [dbo].[JDT1] */
DECLARE FDT TimeStamp;
FDT := /* JDT1."DocDate" */'[%0]';

/* SELECT FROM [dbo].[JDT1] */
DECLARE TDT TimeStamp;
TDT := /* JDT1."DocDate" */'[%1]';

select OACT."AcctCode",OACT."AcctName", OACT."OverCode",OACT."ValidFor",OACT."ValidFrom", case when MC."Amt" is null then 0 else MC."Amt" end as "Motorcycle", case when SP."Amt" is null then 0 else SP."Amt" end as "Spareparts" from OACT
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0001' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") MC on MC."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0002' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") SP on SP."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
Where OACT."Levels" = '6' and OACT."AcctCode" Like '4%%'
union all
select OACT."AcctCode",OACT."AcctName", OACT."OverCode",OACT."ValidFor",OACT."ValidFrom", case when MC."Amt" is null then 0 else MC."Amt" end as "Motorcycle", case when SP."Amt" is null then 0 else SP."Amt" end as "Spareparts" from OACT
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0001' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") MC on MC."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0002' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") SP on SP."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
Where OACT."Levels" = '6' and OACT."AcctCode" Like '5%%'
union all
select OACT."AcctCode",OACT."AcctName", OACT."OverCode",OACT."ValidFor",OACT."ValidFrom", case when MC."Amt" is null then 0 else MC."Amt" end as "Motorcycle", case when SP."Amt" is null then 0 else SP."Amt" end as "Spareparts" from OACT
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0001' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") MC on MC."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0002' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") SP on SP."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
Where OACT."Levels" = '6' and OACT."AcctCode" Like '6%%'
union all
select OACT."AcctCode",OACT."AcctName", OACT."OverCode",OACT."ValidFor",OACT."ValidFrom", case when MC."Amt" is null then 0 else MC."Amt" end as "Motorcycle", case when SP."Amt" is null then 0 else SP."Amt" end as "Spareparts" from OACT
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0001' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") MC on MC."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0002' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") SP on SP."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
Where OACT."Levels" = '6' and OACT."AcctCode" Like '7%%'
union all
select OACT."AcctCode",OACT."AcctName", OACT."OverCode",OACT."ValidFor",OACT."ValidFrom", case when MC."Amt" is null then 0 else MC."Amt" end as "Motorcycle", case when SP."Amt" is null then 0 else SP."Amt" end as "Spareparts" from OACT
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0001' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") MC on MC."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0002' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") SP on SP."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
Where OACT."Levels" = '6' and OACT."AcctCode" Like '8%%'
union all
select OACT."AcctCode",OACT."AcctName", OACT."OverCode",OACT."ValidFor",OACT."ValidFrom", case when MC."Amt" is null then 0 else MC."Amt" end as "Motorcycle", case when SP."Amt" is null then 0 else SP."Amt" end as "Spareparts" from OACT
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0001' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") MC on MC."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
left outer join (select JDT1."Account" as "GLCode", SUM(JDT1."Debit"-JDT1."Credit") as "Amt" from JDT1 where JDT1."RefDate" between :FDT and :TDT and jdt1."ProfitCode" = 'BSG0002' group by JDT1."Account" order by JDT1."Account") SP on SP."GLCode"=OACT."AcctCode"
Where OACT."Levels" = '6' and OACT."AcctCode" Like '9%%'
order by OACT."AcctCode";

Whenever I run this query, its shows different errors like-

  • 'Document' (RDOC)
  • 'User-Defined Values' (CSHS)
  • 'Backend scheduling job' (OBSJ)

But same query running well when I use static dates like -

/* SELECT FROM [dbo].[JDT1] */
DECLARE FDT TimeStamp;
FDT := /* JDT1."DocDate" */'2024.08.01';

/* SELECT FROM [dbo].[JDT1] */
DECLARE TDT TimeStamp;
TDT := /* JDT1."DocDate" */'2024.08.31';

entry.title: Re: ByDesign: Datasource for Production Confirmation incl...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

In data source "Yield & Scrap" (SCMYASU01), you can use other date related filters like "Requested Start Date Year Month" to filter all production lots with requested start date within a specific month, no matter the lot has been finished or not. However, this data source has a limitation: characteristic "Scrap Reason" will always display "Not assigned". The reason behind please refer to KBA: https://me.sap.com/notes/3441476

You can try to join this data source with another data source "Confirmation Journal" (SCMCFJU01) which has valid "Scrap Reason". Rule could be like "Reference Document Type" in SCMCFJU01 is "Production Lot" and "Reference Document ID" in SCMCFJU01 equals to "Production Lot ID" in SCMYASU01.

Henry

SAP Technical Support

entry.title: From Revenue and Demand Planning to MRP Production Planning – A functional view
entry.content:
In this first article, we will explore how revenue planning and volume/demand planning in SAP Analytics Cloud could work together with Material Requirement Planning (MRP) process and production planning in SAP S/4HANA, from the business process point of view, focusing particularly on small and medium enterprises requirements. Understanding this functional integration can help businesses improve efficiency, reduce costs, and achieve their strategic goals. In a next article, we will deep-dive into the technical details of this integration.

The E2E process to cover

In today's competitive market, businesses need efficient planning and execution to stay ahead. At SAP, some services and business applications like SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Business Technology Platform, and SAP S/4HANA offer powerful capabilities to integrate revenue planning with demand planning, production planning and execution, ensuring smooth and effective operations.

  1. Revenue Planning in SAP Analytics Cloud allows businesses to forecast product/services sales, analyze market trends, and create accurate financial plans. This helps companies allocate resources wisely and make informed decisions based on 360° analytics, simulation, and predictive analytics.
  2. Volume/Demand planning in SAP Analytics Cloud involves the process of forecasting and planning the quantities of products or services that will be required to meet customer demand and revenue targets, optimally derived from the Revenue Planning process in the previous step.
  3. Production Planning in Material Requirements Planning (MRP), using Planned Independent Requirements (PIRs) in SAP S/4HANA, ensures that manufacturing processes align with demand forecasts from previous steps. The PIRs are generated based on the demand forecast for the finished products. These PIRs are used as input for the MRP process, which then calculates the material requirements for the finished products' production. The MRP process also takes into account the lead times, inventory levels, and lot sizing rules to determine the quantities and timing of material orders.

In a nutshell, by combining revenue and demand planning (in quantities) with MRP-driven production planning, businesses can better align their operations with market demand. Accurate revenue and demand forecasts from SAP Analytics Cloud inform the MRP process in SAP S/4HANA, leading to efficient production plans and resource use. This end-to-end integration helps reducing production delays, avoid excess inventory, and meet customer demand promptly.

More details

From Revenue Planning ...

As already known from many existing articles, SAP Analytics Cloud is a real toolkit offering unlimited modelling capabilities that enable any type of planning process to be created, and bridging all lines of businesses together (from Finance, to Sales, to HR, to Manufacturing, etc..) in an Extend Planning and Analytics (xP&A) platform. As such, a revenue planning process performed there would involve forecasting future product revenue streams to support strategic decision-making and ensure financial stability.

... to Demand/Volume Planning ...

This above process can also incorporate product pricing information into its modeling. And by having projections of future revenues by product (and other planning axis such as Regions or Customers for instance), as well as the product selling prices, demand in terms of volume can be easily derived (Volume = Revenue/Price). For sure, volume itself can also be planned independently, which would adjust the Revenue accordingly. In this case, we would talk about Consensus Net Revenue. Selling prices can even be simulated along the planning axis, which can also be included in the revenue planning application if needed.

Next, supply chain managers must allocate this volume (as total demand in quantities including inventory requirements) to production sites, considering manufacturing constraints. This step is optimally done in SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP), which can represent a huge added value for large customers with large product portfolio and complex production processes.

But, in this article, we are addressing a simpler view for smaller customers with simpler supply chains. And by leveraging SAP Analytics Cloud, supply chain managers can easily maintain production constraint drivers and leverage the built-in powerful allocation engine to distribute and propagate the demand plan to manufacturing locations. 

… to Production Planning (in S/4 HANA)

Finally, Demand and Supply must reconcile, and the output of this reconciliation must be translated into a production plan, and transferred to SAP S/4HANA, as an expected demand forecast in the form of a Planning Independent Requirement (PIRs). This step is quite important as demand /PIRs represent demand for a product in a given supplying location. So we must ensure that volume plan is issued at the product / manufacturing location level, in order to match the granularity of the PIRs.

As highlighted above, PIRs are primary inputs for the MRP process, and represent anticipated demand for a product that is not directly linked to specific sales orders. During the MRP runs, the system considers PIRs along with actual sales orders and inventory levels to determine net production requirements.

Context

This process may be very effective for small to medium-sized companies with lower production levels and a limited number of SKUs. In fact, smaller companies might require a simpler architectural design in order to achieve this end-to-end process. But as previously mentioned, to optimize this process on a very large scale and seamlessly match unconstrained and constrained plans, incorporating SAP IBP would be optimal.

Components

  • SAP Analytics Cloud
  • SAP BTP Integration Suite
  • Planned Independent Requirement API
  • Planned Independent Requirement Help

entry.title: Returning Excel with a CAP service
entry.content:
Hello, I am trying this feature in order to download a dynamically generated excel from a UI5 app: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/node.js/best-practices#custom-streaming-beta

The way I use it is this:

const readable = Readable.from(buffer)
return {
  value: readable,
  $mediaContentType: 'application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet',
  $mediaContentDispositionFilename: `${fileName}.xlsx`,
}

Where readable is of type stream.Readable and the buffer comes from ExcelJS.

When I trigger it in the browser, it correctly downloads an attachment of type XLSX, but the content is:

{
@odata.context: "$metadata#Edm.Binary"
value: {
  value: "[object Readable]"
  $mediaContentType: "application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet"
  $mediaContentDispositionFilename: "DCT - 212 - TEST TAX - SSD 2024-10-05.xlsx"
  }
}

So the contents of the stream are not actually read, but rather, the stream object is somehow converted into a string. I found a bunch of other community posts regarding media data, but only few of them got clear answers. Am I making a mistake here, or is this a bug? The documentation states no requirements for the streamed data, only that the type of the object must be stream.Readable

Re: Issue with Large Data Extraction from SAP B1 via SAP...
entry.content:
Are you accessing directly to SQL Server?

500k rows should not be an impediment on its own.

If the socket is closed, it could be the one communicating DSP to DPAgent or the one communicating DPAgent to SQL Server.

Have you looked at the DPAgent logs?
It may be a memory problem in the DPAgent, a configured timeout (does it always fail after x minutes?).
It could also be a problem or a timeout in the SQL Server, maybe it is under heavy usage by B1 when you try to do the replication.

Another option you might want to explore is real-time replication.

Regards,

entry.title: Re: Migrate the ASE database (On-Prem) to the SAP hana Cl...
entry.content:
Hi Namit,

My understanding is that the data model in Hana for the ERP may not be identical to the one you have in ASE and thus you want to get the one from ASE and convert it to Hana to load the data from ASE in it, that would be a kind of intermediate data store. Wnen the tool connects to a database server via the gateway it checks that is is not an SAP solution and it complains returning an error message, you can try to use extract offline using DDL files you obtain with any other tool (DDL get may be on possibility) but please keep in mind this is something we have never tried before. As far as I know the data model for an ERP system is huge with thousands of tables (many of them not used) so my suggestion for you is get the DDL only for the tables you want to move data from and the use extract_offline option, they should be converted to Hana with the tool if the extract works well.

Regards.

Javier.

entry.title: Re: Create infoset based on indirect join
entry.content:
Hi!

You should be able to solve this with substring within the join-clause.

Example:

SELECT * FROM usr01
  LEFT JOIN usr02 ON ( SUBSTRING( usr01~bname, 3, 10 ) =  usr02~BNAME )
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_users) .

Cheers,

ben

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - Monitoring on steroids
entry.content:
For improved orientation in the "monitor" section, SAP SCI allows the use of "custom headers" – enabling filtering and searching based on specific values, typically for parameters such as "user ID." This functionality, when implemented in an iflow, greatly simplifies the process of tracking whether a user has passed through the integration or not, eliminating the need to manually search through each execution over a given period.

In practice - Basic usage:

Monitor section:

 

Open „All Artifacts“:

A standard filtering window will pop up. To filter by custom headers, expand using the arrow:

 

Expanded filtering options will be shown; here we are interested in the "Custom Header" field, where we always input the format "Name = Value." For example, with a custom header "ownerIdLog" set to 608306, it would look like this:

 

Now, I can see that user 608306 passed through this integration on September 29th at 12:50, without needing to go through each integration run manually to find the user in the logs.

 

Setting this up in the integration is straightforward, requiring a simple script:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import java.util.HashMap;

Message processData(Message message) {
    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message);
    def headers = message.getHeaders();
    def ownerIdLog = headers.get("ownerIdLog");

    messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("ownerIdLog", ownerIdLog);

    return message;
}

Here, a standard header is retrieved, in which the value 608306 is currently stored with the name "ownerIdLog."

  • The value can be retrieved from anywhere – it doesn’t necessarily need to be set as a header; it could be a property, extracted from the body, or from XML. The important thing is to have the value available in the script.

The key line is:  messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("ownerIdLog", ownerIdLog);

This ensures that the custom header with the name "ownerIdLog" and its value (currently 608306, for example) is added.

In practice - Usage with iterator:

Using It Inside an Iterating Splitter – When the custom header with the same name is written multiple times (such as inside an iterating splitter), it is still possible to search by individual values.

Just put the groovy script mentioned above between interator and gather.

 

The value will be grouped under the same custom header.

For example, searching for "ownerIdLog = 608284" will display the run where the user passed through the integration along with others.

Conclusion

Implementing custom headers in SAP Cloud Integration (SCI) offers significant advantages for monitoring and tracking integration processes. By utilizing custom headers, such as "ownerIdLog," you can streamline the way you filter and search through integration runs, dramatically enhancing your efficiency.

entry.title: Re: Fiori Currency Conversion Issue
entry.content:
Hi @dukejib5  Hmmm... I don't think you have understood SAP currency conversion.

JPY does not have 2 digits following the decimal place. You are correct that it is being stored with 2 decimal places - as is standard - and the displayed correctly in the localized format - i.e. WITHOUT the decimal places. As you have you shown in your screenshots. This is CORRECT as per SAP currency conversion. 

SAP currency conversion maps formats that are NOT the standard 2 digits to the appropriate localization format. That is the difference between how the data is stored and how it is interpreted.  This affects a small but significant number of currencies including JPY, KRW, VND etc. 

This blog might be helpful for you

Currency Decimal issue - JPY, KRW, VND | Localization | Rollout - Japan, Korea, Vietnam

entry.title: solman_setup からのRFC設定「TRUSTING@SID」作成について
entry.content:
solman_setup →管理対象システム→システムのフル設定→管理対象システム設定3「RFC更新」内の設定に下記の差分があります。
・下記の差分はTRUSTING@[SID]の作成に影響がある項目でしょうか。
・下記の差分にてチェックが入っていないことによってどういった影響がございますでしょうか
　・クライアント/更新　SM_SIDCLNT003_READ  へのチェック
　　マニュアル登録されたユーザの受け入れ(ロール割り当てチェック無し)    へのチェック　
　・クライアント/更新　SM_SIDCLNT003_BACK  へのチェック
　　マニュアル登録されたユーザの受け入れ(ロール割り当てチェック無し)   へのチェック

 

　・クライアント/更新　SM_SIDCLNT003_TMW  へのチェック
　　マニュアル登録されたユーザの受け入れ(ロール割り当てチェック無し)   へのチェック

また、solutionmanagerのSM59上にTRUSTING@[SID]が存在する場合としない場合で
solman_setup →管理対象システム→システムのフル設定→管理対象システム設定3「RFC更新」内の設定に下記の差分があります。
・下記の差分はTRUSTING@[SID]の作成に影響がある項目でしょうか。
・下記の差分にてチェックが入っていないことによってどういった影響がございますでしょうか
　・クライアント/更新　SM_SIDCLNT003_READ  へのチェック
　　マニュアル登録されたユーザの受け入れ(ロール割り当てチェック無し)    へのチェック
　　
　・クライアント/更新　SM_SIDCLNT003_BACK  へのチェック
　　マニュアル登録されたユーザの受け入れ(ロール割り当てチェック無し)   へのチェック

 

　・クライアント/更新　SM_SIDCLNT003_TMW  へのチェック
　　マニュアル登録されたユーザの受け入れ(ロール割り当てチェック無し)   へのチェック

 

詳細は添付ファイル「20240919_solman_setup設定」


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: solman_setup からのRFC設定「TRUSTING@SID」作成について
entry.content:
ご担当者　様

いつもお世話になっております。

・下記の差分はTRUSTING@[SID]の作成に影響がありません。

  • SM_SIDCLNT003_READ
  • SM_SIDCLNT003_BACK
  • SM_SIDCLNT003_TMW 

・チェックが入っていないことによってどういった影響がございますでしょうか。

このステップで登録できるRFCと生成される場所および用途は以下のとおりです。チェックが入っていなければ下記の機能利用に影響がございます。

SM_<SID>CLNT<Clint番号>_READ: T-cd SMSYからリモートでシステムデータを読み込む場合や、システム監視用のデータ収集メソッドを読み込む場合

SM_<SID>CLNT<Clint番号>_TMW: 変更依頼管理(ChaRM)、ジョブ監視、その他の機能 

SM_<SID>CLNT<Clint番号>_BACK: 管理対象システムからSAP Solution Managerシステムにサービスデータを送信する場合や、Solmanからサービス定義を取得 する場合 

以上、宜しくお願い致します。

SAPサポート

entry.title: Re: Advice on Reducing DHCDC_RTSTORE Table Size in S/4HANA
entry.content:
DHCDC_RTSTORE table in S/4HANA is primarily used to store data related to the real-time replication. While old data in this table can usually be deleted as part of cleanup activities, it’s essential to evaluate its relevance based on your needs.

you can use ABAP or SQL

e.g: DELETE FROM DHCDC_RTSTORE WHERE TIMESTAMP < (CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90' DAY);

Replace 90 with the desired number of days based on your retention policy.

You can ask ABAPER or BASIS to create a background job in S/4HANA to automate this deletion process.

 

Thanks

Savio

entry.title: Premium Hub CoE – DSC Knowledge Bits – New Migration Guide LE-WM to EWM on S/4HANA
entry.content:
In this blog, I would like to share the latest updates on the new migration guide that was released for supporting customers and consultants during planning the migration from SAP ERP LE-WM or SAP S/4HANA Stock Room Management to EWM on S/4HANA. 

Additionally, I would like to clear up some confusion about end dates of usage rights and mainstream maintenance for SAP LE-WM.

LE-WM Roadmap - Important Dates:
Let's start with the SAP LE-WM roadmap and timeline, as this is mainly dependent on the ERP solution release on which LE-WM is running.

  • LE-WM on ERP ECC:
    SAP will provide mainstream maintenance until 31.12.2027 for SAP Business Suite 7 core applications, refer to SAP Note 2881788 for details.

  • LE-WM on ERP S/4HANA:
    SAP restricts the usage of components which are in a compatibility mode until 31.12.2025, refer to SAP Notes 2269324, 2881166, and 2577428 for details.

Migration Guide Overview:
In this migration guide you will find information about:

  • General Process Changes between LE-WM and EWM
  • System Setup Guides
  • Organizational Structure
  • Customizing Settings for Product Master
  • Define Handling Unit Management
  • Storage Bin Migration
  • Putaway and Stock Removal
  • Material Master Migration
  • Transactional Data Migration
  • Stock Migration
  • Physical Inventory Completeness Migration

Out of Scope:

  • In general, transactional data is not transferred from LE-WM to EWM. So no documents can be open when the migration starts. For more information, see Transactional Data Migration.
  • Existing RF transactions are not transferred automatically from LE-WM to EWM.
  • System users and the related authorization roles and profiles are not transferred automatically from LE-WM to EWM.
  • The existing migration tools do not migrate Customizing. Due to the broader functionality in EWM, the Customizing differs from LE-WM. For more information, see General Process Changes between LE-WM and EWM.
  • Customer-specific objects are not transferred

SAP Note link where you can download the mgiration guide: https://me.sap.com/notes/3523591.

Thanks to the EWM product management and CoE teams for the preparation of this useful document.

Learn more about SAP Premium Engagement and how a global team of experts can help you to get the most out of your SAP solutions.

If you already are a Premium Engagement customer and you are interested in knowing more about SAP S/4HANA Supply Chain Extended Warehouse Management solution, contact your Technical Quality Manager (TQM) to arrange for Architecture Point of View (APoV) service.

#CoE EMEA SCM1

entry.title: Announcing Global Availability of the Content Generation Assistant in SAP Automation Pilot!
entry.content:
Announcing General Availability of the Content Generation Assistant in SAP Automation Pilot!

We’re excited to announce that our Generative AI (GenAI) feature in SAP Automation Pilot has officially reached General Availability globally as of 20th September! 

This is a major milestone for our team and a significant step toward delivering cutting-edge functionality to our customers. It has been a long journey filled with challenges, but we’ve made it - and the future of automation looks brighter than ever.

What's New for the Content Generation Assistant?

With the latest updates, the Content Generation Assistant now offers the ability to generate scheduled executions within SAP Automation Pilot with just a single prompt. The assistant automatically creates ready-to-use executions that are scheduled to run at specific times, streamlining your workflow and reducing manual effort. This new feature adds more flexibility and control, making operations more efficient and easier to manage.

Enabling Content Generation Assistant

Activate the feature for AI-based generation of commands, executors, inputs, and scheduled executions.

Prerequisites

To enable the Generative AI capabilities, you need to have the GenAI role in your subaccount. For more information, see Permissions and Roles.

Procedure

Follow the steps below to enable or disable the AI-based generation of commands, executors, inputs, and scheduled executions.

1. From the sidebar navigation, choose Terms and Conditions.

2. Enable the Content Generation Assistant by switching on the toggle button.

3. Read and understand the terms and conditions for the usage of AI-based feature. Select the I have read and understood the terms and conditions checkbox at the bottom of the pop-up to provide your consent.

4. Choose Accept.

Result

The Content Generation Assistant is now enabled and you can generate AI-based commands, inputs, and scheduled executions. 

Try It Now!

We invite all our customers to experience the power of the Content Generation Assistant firsthand. Activate it in your SAP Automation Pilot today and start generating automation flows with ease. Whether you're looking to streamline operations, improve efficiency, or expand your automation capabilities, this tool is designed to make it happen.

To see GenAI in action, check out our How-to blog post, which guides you through setting up and using the Content Generation Assistant effectively. Additionally, in a recent success story, Sierra Digital demonstrates how they utilized the GenAI capabilities of SAP Automation Pilot to streamline SAP ECC to S/4HANA migrations, enhancing code compatibility assessments and clean core conversions.

Conclusion

The release of the Content Generation Assistant marks just the beginning of our efforts to incorporate GenAI into SAP Automation Pilot. By making automation smarter and more intuitive, we’re enabling customers to achieve greater efficiency and agility in their operations. We’re eager to see how this capability transforms the way our customers approach automation, and we look forward to continuing to innovate in this space.

Stay tuned for more updates as we push the boundaries of automation with GenAI!

entry.title: What is the tcode for finding the warehouse number under a plant code ?
entry.content: What is the tcode for finding the warehouse number under a plant code ?

entry.title: Re: Rental of equipments
entry.content:
Greetings,

you can define this by having several maintenance plants assigned to one maintancce planning plant. Your equipment would be part of the plant and could migrate between plants with relocation. If you want to change maintenance plant you first need to uninstall equipment from the functional location and then change maintancce plant (and possibly additional plant relevant data)

 

You can simplify this scenario if you do not require multiple maintance plants - you can have functional locations which would represent plants and you can have these equipment migrating between them.

Best regards, Matjaž

entry.title: Preliminary What’s New Information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408.2
entry.content:
Preliminary versions of What’s New information and of the Product Assistance are now available for customers and partners to get a preview on the upcoming new and changed features of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408.2.

You can find this preview on the current Help Portal page SAP S/4HANA Cloud (see What’s New section “Preview of SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2408.2”). To access the Preliminary What’s New information, please use the What’s New Viewer or the Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD) tool.

The RASD tool provides you with a personalized and tailored view on What’s New information based on the activated and used scope within your system. The preliminary information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2408.2 will be available in RASD within the next few days. We recommend using RASD to prepare for upgrades and updates of your system and to focus on the new and changed features that are relevant for you. A valid S-User ID is needed to use the RASD tool and to view all the preliminary content stated above.

In this release, try the new Commenting Feature for What's New documentation. See this blog for more information.
